hypothesis: When the CCI reaches extreme values and the StochRSI confirms overbought or oversold conditions, the price tends to revert to its mean in low‑volatility periods. Using ATR for dynamic stop‑loss and take‑profit ensures risk is scaled to current market conditions, improving survivability.

When CCI reaches extreme levels the price tends to revert, and a complementary extreme in Stoch RSI confirms the overextension. Filtering entries to periods with ATR above a minimum ensures sufficient volatility, reducing whipsaw losses in low‑vol markets. Exiting when CCI crosses the zero line or Stoch RSI returns to mid‑range captures the mean reversion before further drift, improving Sharpe and reducing drawdown.

When price reaches the extreme CCI levels, the market tends to revert; coupling this with StochRSI confirming overbought or oversold conditions filters out weak extremes and increases probability of a reversal back to the CCI neutral zone.

Extreme CCI values combined with complementary StochRSI overbought/oversold levels capture true mean‑reversion impulses; tightening the exit to a CCI ±20 band reduces whipsaws, while ATR‑based sizing adapts to volatility and limits drawdown.

When price reaches an extreme CCI level while the StochRSI confirms overbought or oversold conditions, the market tends to revert toward the CCI mean; using ATR as a volatility filter ensures entries occur only in sufficiently liquid periods, reducing noise and improving risk-adjusted returns.

Mean‑reversion signals are stronger when price extremes detected by CCI coincide with oscillator extremes from StochRSI and confirmed by volume pressure from MFI; this combination reduces entries in weak‑volume or trending regimes while ATR provides a volatility‑scaled risk framework.
